body_txt: A form of insanity far more common in fiction than reality, the delusion that one is a famous person, or at the very least the modern reincarnation of same. In real life, this is a subcategory of grandiose delusion, a belief that one is far more famous, wealthy, or powerful than in reality. It can take many forms, but the idea of claiming to be a famous person is its most typical form in fiction, simply because it's very obviously absurd and untrue. Napoléon Bonaparte is the most commonly used person for this. In real life, this is due to much of the foundation of modern psychiatry having been laid down in the early 19th century, with France having been a major center of it due to the work of Philippe Pinel. Sufferers of grandiose delusion tend to associate themselves with mythologized figures, societal outcasts, or extreme personalities, and Napoleon was all three—and in post-Revolution France, he was certainly looming large in the public imagination. As a result, early psychiatric reports frequently described patients claiming to be Napoleon, and the stereotype has stuck, even though him no longer being a relevant figure means that GD patients rarely identify as him in the modern day. God and Jesus are also frequently seen, though with the rise of mass media since the late 19th and early 20th century those afflicted with such delusions are just as likely to be inspired by fictional figures as real ones. Besides being a visual shorthand for insanity, some may even invoke this by dressing as Napoleon when appearing before an authority figure (parents, a school principal, a judge, etc) to avoid punishment . This trope is usually played for comedy, but can also be a bittersweet commentary on contemporary society. It may also be used to bring extra hilarity to a person already suffering from Identity Amnesia . Not to be confused with the trope The Napoleon or The Napoleon Complex, where a short person is hostile or violent. Compare A God Am I . Contrast Thoroughly Mistaken Identity and Dr. Psych Patient . See also Daydream Believer and Otherkin , for people in real life or otherwise who believe they are reincarnations or representations of mythic/fictional characters, animals, or other things, obviously usually with much less delusion than in fiction. Note that in some religious sects people believe they are reincarnations of, vessels for, or possessed by spiritual beings, such as the Horse of Samedi in Haitian Voudoun . These are not the same as delusions, according to psychology and social scientists. People who are delusional usually perceive things which aren't there, fail to perceive things which are there, and exhibit other traits like psychotic rage, extreme narcissism or paranoia. Someone who holds beliefs that don't make sense isn't automatically delusional , despite the common usage of that word. For cases where a character sarcastically claims to be someone famous, see And I'm the Queen of Sheba .
